docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Software testing generates large amounts of data — test results and system logs — that are typically scattered across separate files and tools. Correlating them manually is tedious and error-prone.

cetk-lt (comlet Embedded TestKit Log Transformer) solves this by ingesting Robot Framework (RF) test output from cetk-cli and additional log sources into a shared TimescaleDB database, correlated by time, where any PostgreSQL-compatible visualization tool — such as Grafana — makes cross-source patterns visible.

Key ConceptsKey FeaturesExample Dashboards
  • RF output.xml → transformed and inserted into TimescaleDB
  • Additional log sources → transformed and inserted into TimescaleDB
  • All timestamps normalized to UTC before insertion
  • Results visualized in any PostgreSQL-compatible tool (e.g., Grafana dashboards)
  • Two subcommands: cetk-lt rf and cetk-lt log
  • Unix-style piping between subcommands
  • Flexible timestamp and timezone handling
  • Extensible: new log sources can be added without changing existing pipelines

The following screenshots show Grafana as one example of a PostgreSQL-compatible visualization tool built on cetk-lt data.

Job Overview — all test runs and jobs at a glance

Test Run & Suite Overview — drill into timing and results per test run

docs.title: Piping Subcommands
docs.text: cetk-lt supports Unix-style piping between subcommands. After each successful run, cetk-lt prints the normalized UTC test_run_starttime to stdout (see Timestamp & Timezone for the exact format), which the next command reads from stdin.

docs.title: Sequential Piping
docs.text:
Run rf first, then pipe its output into one or more log calls:

cetk-lt rf <path> <job> <db_name> [options] \
  | cetk-lt log mosquitto <path> <db_name> [options]
  • rf determines the test_run_starttime (from --test-run-starttime or auto-detected from output.xml) and prints it to stdout.
  • Each log call reads test_run_starttime from stdin if it is not provided as a positional argument, then prints the same value to stdout.
  • This allows chaining as many sources as needed without repeating the timestamp.

To chain multiple log sources, extend the pipe:

cetk-lt rf <path> <job> <db_name> [options] \
  | cetk-lt log mosquitto <path1> <db_name> [options] \
  | cetk-lt log mosquitto <path2> <db_name> [options]

docs.title: Concurrent Execution
docs.text:
If you provide test_run_starttime explicitly to every command, you can run them in parallel (e.g. in separate CI/CD steps or background processes):

STARTTIME="2024-01-01 12:00:00.000000Z"

cetk-lt rf <path> <job> <db_name> -t "$STARTTIME" [options] &
cetk-lt log mosquitto <path> <db_name> "$STARTTIME" [options] &
wait

Note

When running concurrently, all commands must use the same test_run_starttime so that all data is assigned to the same test run in the database.

docs.title: Timestamp & Timezone
docs.text: cetk-lt normalizes all timestamps to UTC before inserting them into the database. This page describes the accepted input formats and the timezone handling rules.

docs.title: Accepted Formats for test_run_starttime
docs.text:
The following formats are accepted wherever a test_run_starttime is expected (the --test-run-starttime option of cetk-lt rf and the positional argument of cetk-lt log):

Format Example Type %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%f 2024-01-01 12:00:00.000000 Naive (no timezone) %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%fZ 2024-01-01 12:00:00.000000Z UTC (RFC 3339) %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%f+/-HH:MM 2024-01-01 12:00:00.000000+01:00 UTC offset (RFC 3339)

docs.title: Naive Timezone Options
docs.text:
When a timestamp carries no timezone information (a "naive" timestamp), two options let you specify which timezone to assume:

  • --test-run-starttime-naive-tz — timezone for a naive test_run_starttime (cetk-lt rf, cetk-lt log)
  • --source-log-naive-tz — timezone for naive timestamps in the source log file (cetk-lt rf, cetk-lt log)

Accepted formats for both:

Format Example IANA timezone name Europe/Berlin, America/New_York, Asia/Tokyo Fixed UTC offset +01:00, +02:00, -05:00

Note

IANA names account for DST automatically, but cetk-lt will fail if the given timestamp falls on an ambiguous DST transition — use a fixed offset in that case.

docs.title: Precedence Rules for test_run_starttime
docs.text:
The following rules apply to the --test-run-starttime option (cetk-lt rf) and the positional test_run_starttime argument (cetk-lt log):

  1. If the timestamp already contains timezone information (Z or +/-HH:MM), that timezone is used and --test-run-starttime-naive-tz is ignored.
  2. If the timestamp is naive and --test-run-starttime-naive-tz is provided, that timezone is assumed and a warning is logged.
  3. If the timestamp is naive and no --test-run-starttime-naive-tz is provided, UTC is assumed and a warning is logged.
  4. In cetk-lt rf mode, if --test-run-starttime-naive-tz is provided without --test-run-starttime, the option is ignored and a warning is logged.
  5. If --test-run-starttime-naive-tz points to an IANA timezone and the local naive timestamp is ambiguous or non-existent at a DST transition, cetk-lt fails and asks for an explicit fixed offset (e.g. +01:00).

docs.title: Precedence Rules for Source Log Timestamps
docs.text:
The following rules apply to timestamps found within the source log file, controlled by --source-log-naive-tz (cetk-lt rf, cetk-lt log):

  1. If a source timestamp already contains timezone information, that timezone is used.
  2. If the source timestamp is naive and --source-log-naive-tz is provided, that timezone is assumed.
  3. If the source timestamp is naive and no --source-log-naive-tz is provided, UTC is assumed.
  4. If --source-log-naive-tz is provided but all parsed source timestamps are already timezone-aware, cetk-lt logs a single DEBUG message that the option was not applied.

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
All emitted test_run_starttime values are printed to stdout as UTC with a Z suffix:

2024-01-01 01:01:01.111000Z

This format can be piped directly as input to a subsequent cetk-lt log call without any ambiguity. See Piping for details.

docs.title: Command Line Interface
docs.text:
cetk-lt is a command line interface for transforming log data from RF test runs and additional log sources into a TimescaleDB database for visualization and analysis with any PostgreSQL-compatible tool (e.g., Grafana).

Its purpose is to act as the bridge between test execution artifacts and the visualization layer — taking raw output files and inserting their data in a structured, queryable form into the database.

It is designed to be used both manually after a test run and within a CI/CD pipeline as an automated post-processing step. Two subcommands cover the currently supported log sources:

  • cetk-lt rf — transforms a Robot Framework output.xml file
  • cetk-lt log — transforms additional log sources

The subcommands can be run independently or chained via Unix-style piping. All timestamps are normalized to UTC before insertion, regardless of the source timezone. See Timestamp & Timezone for details.

For general usage information, cetk-lt provides a built-in help:

cetk-lt --help

as well as help for every subcommand:

cetk-lt rf --help
cetk-lt log --help

See Installation to get started and Global Options for options that apply to all subcommands.

Exit Codes Code Meaning 0 Success 1 Invalid argument or timestamp format 2 Invalid CLI argument — missing required argument, invalid choice, or bad format 250 Internal argument error 251 Transformation validation failed 252 Input file not found 253 RF model compatibility issue — re-run with --verbose 254 Type error in argument processing 255 Unexpected error — re-run with --verbose for details

docs.title: Global Options
docs.text:
Global options must be placed before the subcommand.

cetk-lt [global option] [sub-command] ...

docs.title: --help / -h
docs.text: Show help/usage details and exit.

docs.title: --version
docs.text: Show cetk-lt's version and exit.

docs.title: --verbose / -v
docs.text:
Show detailed debug messages on stderr (DEBUG level).

  • Default: disabled

docs.title: Installing cetk-lt
docs.text:
cetk-lt is available as a Python wheel package and can be installed by its name "cetk-lt" with e.g. pip.

pip install cetk-lt

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text:
• Python ≥ 3.10
  • TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL-compatible) instance with the cetk-lt schema already deployed

Note

cetk-lt inserts data into pre-existing tables. It does not create the database or the schema. The target database and all required tables must exist before running cetk-lt.

Do you need a TimescaleDB instance with a cetk-lt schema?

We are happy to assist you if you need a TimescaleDB instance with schema implementation. Feel free to contact our sales for further details.

docs.title: Transforming Additional Log Sources
docs.text:
Transforms and inserts additional log data from supported sources into the database.

cetk-lt [global options] log <source> <path> <db_name> [test_run_starttime] [options]

After a successful run, cetk-lt prints the normalized UTC test_run_starttime to stdout (e.g. 2024-01-01 01:01:01.111000Z), which can be piped into a subsequent cetk-lt log call.

docs.title: source
docs.text:
Log source type to transform. The source determines the expected log format.

Available sources:

  • mosquitto — Mosquitto MQTT broker log
Do you need a different log source?

cetk-lt is designed to be extensible — additional log sources can be integrated without changing existing pipelines. Feel free to contact our sales for further details.

docs.title: path
docs.text: Path to the log file.

docs.title: db_name
docs.text: Name of the target database.

docs.title: test_run_starttime
docs.text:
Start timestamp of the test run. Used to assign log data to a specific test run.

  • Format: %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%f with optional timezone suffix Z or +/-HH:MM
  • If omitted, cetk-lt reads the value from stdin (e.g. piped from cetk-lt rf)

Note

test_run_starttime is required — either provide it as a positional argument or pipe it from a preceding cetk-lt rf or cetk-lt log call. The [...] notation reflects that it may be omitted when piped via stdin. See Piping for details.

See Timestamp & Timezone for accepted formats and timezone handling.

docs.title: --help / -h
docs.text: Show help message and exit.

docs.title: --test-run-starttime-naive-tz (str)
docs.text:
Timezone to assume for the test_run_starttime value — the single timestamp that identifies the test run in the database.

  • Accepted formats: IANA name (e.g. Europe/Berlin) or fixed offset (e.g. +01:00) — see Timestamp & Timezone for DST behavior
  • Default: None (UTC assumed if no timezone info is present)

docs.title: --source-log-naive-tz (str)
docs.text:
Timezone to assume for the individual event timestamps inside the source file.

  • Accepted formats: IANA name (e.g. Europe/Berlin) or fixed offset (e.g. +01:00) — see Timestamp & Timezone for DST behavior
  • Default: None (UTC assumed if no timezone info is present)
Difference between the two timezone options
  • --test-run-starttime-naive-tz — applies to the test run identifier: one timestamp per run, used as the grouping key in the database
  • --source-log-naive-tz — applies to every event timestamp inside the source log file (e.g. keywords/suites in RF XML, individual lines in a log source)

Both options are independent and can carry different timezones — for example when the CI server runs in UTC but the source log file was generated on a Europe/Berlin machine.

docs.title: --host (str)
docs.text:
IP address or DNS name of the database host.

  • Default: "" (local socket)

docs.title: --port (int)
docs.text:
Port of the database.

  • Default: 5432

docs.title: --username / -u (str)
docs.text:
Username for the database login.

  • Default: ""

docs.title: --password / -p (str)
docs.text:
Password for the database login.

  • Default: ""

docs.title: --log-timestamp-format / -ltf (str)
docs.text:
Custom Python strptime format string for the timestamp in the log file.

  • Default: "" (auto-detection)
Auto-detected formats (Mosquitto)

When -ltf is not provided, cetk-lt auto-detects the timestamp format from the first line of the log file. The following formats are supported:

  • Unix epoch seconds: 1234567890: <message>
  • ISO-style: 2024-01-01T12:00:00: <message> (optional fractional seconds and timezone offset)

Use -ltf when the log file uses a different format. The timestamp must be followed by : (colon and space).

docs.title: Transforming Robot Framework Output
docs.text:
Transforms and inserts Robot Framework data from an output.xml file into the database.

cetk-lt [global options] rf <path> <job> <db_name> [options]

After a successful run, cetk-lt prints the normalized UTC test_run_starttime to stdout (e.g. 2024-01-01 01:01:01.111000Z), which can be piped into a subsequent cetk-lt log call.

docs.title: path
docs.text: Path to the Robot Framework output.xml source file — requires output.xml schema version 5.

docs.title: job
docs.text: Name of the job that was tested. Used to identify the test run in the database.

docs.title: db_name
docs.text: Name of the target database.

docs.title: --help / -h
docs.text: Show help message and exit.

docs.title: --test-run-starttime / -t (str)
docs.text:
Start timestamp of the test run.

  • Format: %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%f with optional timezone suffix Z or +/-HH:MM
  • Default: auto-detected from the first suite in output.xml

Note

If omitted, cetk-lt reads the start timestamp from the first suite found in output.xml. The detected timestamp is normalized to UTC and printed to stdout.

See Timestamp & Timezone for accepted formats and timezone handling.

docs.title: --test-run-starttime-naive-tz (str)
docs.text:
Timezone to assume for the test_run_starttime value — the single timestamp that identifies the test run in the database.

  • Accepted formats: IANA name (e.g. Europe/Berlin) or fixed offset (e.g. +01:00) — see Timestamp & Timezone for DST behavior
  • Default: None (UTC assumed if no timezone info is present)

docs.title: --source-log-naive-tz (str)
docs.text:
Timezone to assume for the individual event timestamps inside the source file.

  • Accepted formats: IANA name (e.g. Europe/Berlin) or fixed offset (e.g. +01:00) — see Timestamp & Timezone for DST behavior
  • Default: None (UTC assumed if no timezone info is present)
Difference between the two timezone options
  • --test-run-starttime-naive-tz — applies to the test run identifier: one timestamp per run, used as the grouping key in the database
  • --source-log-naive-tz — applies to every event timestamp inside the source log file (e.g. keywords/suites in RF XML, individual lines in a log source)

Both options are independent and can carry different timezones — for example when the CI server runs in UTC but the source log file was generated on a Europe/Berlin machine.

docs.title: --host (str)
docs.text:
IP address or DNS name of the database host.

  • Default: "" (local socket)

docs.title: --port (int)
docs.text:
Port of the database.

  • Default: 5432

docs.title: --username / -u (str)
docs.text:
Username for the database login.

  • Default: ""

docs.title: --password / -p (str)
docs.text:
Password for the database login.

  • Default: ""

docs.title: --log-link (str)
docs.text:
URL to the Robot Framework log.html artifact — stored in the database and used to link back to the RF log from visualization dashboards.

  • Default: "" (no link stored)

docs.title: --report-link (str)
docs.text:
URL to the Robot Framework report.html artifact — stored in the database and used to link back to the RF report from visualization dashboards.

  • Default: "" (no link stored)
Exit Codes

See Command Line Interface for the complete exit code reference.

docs.title: RF Compatibility
docs.text:
cetk-lt supports output.xml schema version 5, introduced with Robot Framework 7.0.

Note

output.xml files generated by Robot Framework < 7.0 use an earlier schema version and are not supported.

RF version Status 7.x Supported 8.x Not validated < 7.0 Not supported

docs.title: Thirdparty OSS
docs.text:
A list of third-party OSS software used to build this application (incl. a reference to their license)

  • robotframework - Apache License 2.0
  • psycopg2-binary - LGPL with exceptions
  • tzdata - Apache License 2.0

docs.text:
IP address or DNS name of the database host.

  • Default: "" (local socket)

docs.title: --port (int)
docs.text:
Port of the database.

  • Default: 5432

docs.title: --username / -u (str)
docs.text:
Username for the database login.

  • Default: ""

docs.title: --password / -p (str)
docs.text:
Password for the database login.

  • Default: ""

docs.title: --test-run-starttime-naive-tz (str)
docs.text:
Timezone to assume for the test_run_starttime value — the single timestamp that identifies the test run in the database.

  • Accepted formats: IANA name (e.g. Europe/Berlin) or fixed offset (e.g. +01:00) — see Timestamp & Timezone for DST behavior
  • Default: None (UTC assumed if no timezone info is present)

docs.title: --source-log-naive-tz (str)
docs.text:
Timezone to assume for the individual event timestamps inside the source file.

  • Accepted formats: IANA name (e.g. Europe/Berlin) or fixed offset (e.g. +01:00) — see Timestamp & Timezone for DST behavior
  • Default: None (UTC assumed if no timezone info is present)
Difference between the two timezone options
  • --test-run-starttime-naive-tz — applies to the test run identifier: one timestamp per run, used as the grouping key in the database
  • --source-log-naive-tz — applies to every event timestamp inside the source log file (e.g. keywords/suites in RF XML, individual lines in a log source)

Both options are independent and can carry different timezones — for example when the CI server runs in UTC but the source log file was generated on a Europe/Berlin machine.

docs.title: Timezone naive tz note
docs.text:
Difference between the two timezone options
  • --test-run-starttime-naive-tz — applies to the test run identifier: one timestamp per run, used as the grouping key in the database
  • --source-log-naive-tz — applies to every event timestamp inside the source log file (e.g. keywords/suites in RF XML, individual lines in a log source)

Both options are independent and can carry different timezones — for example when the CI server runs in UTC but the source log file was generated on a Europe/Berlin machine.
